subject: ap-chemistry
title: Silicon Chemistry: Atomic Structure, IMF, Entropy & Photon Energy
stimulus: Questions about the element Si (silicon, Z = 14) and some of its compounds.

parts.prompt:
The mass spectrum of a pure sample of Si is shown below.
(i) How many protons and how many neutrons are in the nucleus of an atom of the most abundant isotope of Si?
(ii) Write the ground-state electron configuration of Si.
parts.scoring_notes:
(i) From the mass spectrum, the most abundant isotope is Si-28. Silicon has atomic number 14 (14 protons). Mass number = protons + neutrons → 28 = 14 + neutrons → 14 neutrons.
(ii) Si has 14 electrons. Fill orbitals in order: 1s²2s²2p⁶3s²3p². Also accepted: [Ne]3s²3p².

scoring_points.description: For 14 protons and 14 neutrons (most abundant isotope Si-28): 1 point

required_elements: 14 protons and 14 neutrons (for Si-28, the tallest peak in the mass spectrum)
alternatives.correct_example: The most abundant isotope is Si-28 (tallest peak at mass 28). Si has atomic number 14 → 14 protons. Neutrons = mass number − atomic number = 28 − 14 = 14 neutrons.

wrong_examples: Saying 14 protons and 15 neutrons (Si-29) or 14 protons and 16 neutrons (Si-30) — read the tallest peak in the mass spectrum for the most abundant isotope.
Confusing atomic number with mass number — Z = 14 gives protons; neutrons = mass number − Z.
common_traps: Most abundant isotope = tallest bar in mass spectrum. For Si: Si-28 is most abundant. Z = 14 protons. Neutrons = A − Z = 28 − 14 = 14.
scoring_points.official_rubric: For the correct answer: 1 point. 14 protons and 14 neutrons (most abundant isotope is Si-28 from mass spectrum).

sample_responses.commentary: Matches SG answer; correctly identifies Si-28 as the tallest peak and subtracts Z to get neutrons.
Si-29 is not the most abundant isotope; the tallest peak in the spectrum is at mass 28.

scoring_points.description: For the correct ground-state electron configuration of Si: 1 point
wrong_examples: Writing [Ne]3s²3p⁴ — too many p electrons (Si is Group 14, only 2 electrons in 3p).
Writing [Ne]3s¹3p³ — this violates the Aufbau principle; 3s fills before 3p.
common_traps: Si is Period 3, Group 14 → [Ne] core + 3s²3p². The 3p subshell has only 2 electrons in Si (not 4 or 6). Hund's rule: 2 separate 3p orbitals each have 1 electron.
scoring_points.official_rubric: For the correct answer: 1 point. Accept 1s$^2$2s$^2$2p$^6$3s$^2$3p$^2$ or [Ne]3s$^2$3p$^2$.

sample_responses.commentary: Correct full electron configuration for Si (Z = 14).
Correct noble-gas abbreviated configuration; both forms accepted by SG.
3p$^4$ gives Si 16 electrons total; Si has only 14 electrons.

parts.prompt: At 161 K, SiH₄ boils but SiO₂ remains as a solid. Using principles of interparticle forces, explain the difference in boiling points.
parts.scoring_notes: SiH₄ is a molecular compound with only weak London dispersion forces between molecules → low boiling point (161 K). SiO₂ is a covalent network solid with strong Si–O covalent bonds extending throughout the entire three-dimensional lattice. To boil SiO₂, many covalent Si–O bonds must be broken simultaneously, requiring extremely high temperatures (BP ≈ 2950 K). The difference in interparticle forces (weak LDF in SiH₄ vs strong network covalent bonds in SiO₂) explains the enormous BP difference.

scoring_points.description: For correctly identifying the interparticle forces in both compounds and explaining the BP difference: 1 point

required_elements: SiH₄ has only London dispersion forces (weak, between molecules).
SiO₂ is a covalent network solid with strong covalent Si–O bonds throughout the lattice.
Network covalent bonds are much stronger than LDF → SiO₂ has far higher BP.
alternatives.correct_example: SiH₄ is a molecular compound held together only by weak London dispersion forces (LDF) between SiH₄ molecules. Little energy is required to overcome these weak forces, so SiH₄ boils at 161 K. SiO₂ is a covalent network solid in which each Si atom is covalently bonded to four O atoms in a continuous three-dimensional lattice. Boiling requires breaking many strong Si–O covalent bonds, which requires much more energy. Therefore SiO₂ has a far higher boiling point than SiH₄.

wrong_examples: Saying SiO₂ has 'stronger LDF' — SiO₂ is not a molecular substance; it does not have London dispersion forces between molecules.
Saying SiH₄ has hydrogen bonding — Si–H bonds are not polar enough and Si is not electronegative enough to form H-bonds; only LDF exist.
common_traps: Key distinction: SiH₄ = molecular (weak LDF); SiO₂ = covalent network solid (strong covalent bonds throughout). Never confuse network covalent solids with ionic or molecular substances when explaining BP.
scoring_points.official_rubric: For a correct explanation: 1 point. $\text{SiH}_4$ is composed of molecules with only London dispersion forces. $\text{SiO}_2$ is a network covalent compound with covalent Si–O bonds. London dispersion forces are much weaker than covalent bonds, so $\text{SiH}_4$ boils at a much lower temperature.

sample_responses.response: $\text{SiH}_4$ is a molecular compound with only London dispersion forces between molecules. $\text{SiO}_2$ is a covalent network solid; boiling requires breaking strong covalent Si–O bonds extending throughout the lattice. Since covalent bonds are much stronger than LDF, $\text{SiO}_2$ has a much higher boiling point.
sample_responses.commentary: Identifies both force types and directly contrasts their relative strengths; matches SG reasoning.

sample_responses.response: $\text{SiO}_2$ has stronger London dispersion forces because it has more electrons.
sample_responses.commentary: $\text{SiO}_2$ is not a molecular substance; LDF between molecules does not apply.

parts.prompt: At high temperatures, SiH₄ decomposes to form solid silicon and hydrogen gas. Write a balanced equation for the reaction.
parts.scoring_notes: SiH₄(g) → Si(s) + 2H₂(g). Balancing: 1 Si on each side; 4 H on left = 2 H₂ on right. State symbols not required.

scoring_points.description: For the correct balanced equation: SiH₄(g) → Si(s) + 2H₂(g): 1 point
wrong_examples: Writing SiH₄ → Si + H₄ — hydrogen must form H₂ (the diatomic elemental form).
Writing SiH₄ → SiH₂ + H₂ — the prompt specifies solid silicon is the product, not a silicon hydride.
common_traps: Products: solid silicon (Si(s)) and hydrogen gas (H₂(g), the elemental form). Balance: 4 H in SiH₄ requires 2 H₂ molecules.
scoring_points.official_rubric: For the correct balanced equation (state symbols not required): 1 point. $\text{SiH}_4(g) \rightarrow \text{Si}(s) + 2\text{H}_2(g)$.

sample_responses.commentary: Correctly balanced; hydrogen appears as diatomic $\text{H}_2$.
$\text{H}_4$ is not the elemental form of hydrogen; must be $2\text{H}_2$.

parts.prompt: Using the data in the table below (S°: H₂(g) = 131, Si(s) = 18, SiH₄(g) = 205 J/mol·K), explain why the absolute molar entropy of Si(s) is less than that of H₂(g).
parts.scoring_notes: H₂(g) molecules are gas phase: they can freely translate, rotate, and have a wide range of momenta across a large volume → high entropy. Si(s) atoms are locked in fixed lattice positions with only vibrational motion → very limited positional disorder. Gases always have greater entropy than solids (at the same temperature) because gas-phase particles have much greater freedom of motion and are far more dispersed.

scoring_points.description: For correctly identifying that gas-phase H₂ has more positional/translational freedom than solid Si: 1 point

required_elements: H₂(g) molecules are highly dispersed in space and have translational/rotational freedom (greater disorder).
Si(s) atoms are confined to fixed lattice positions with only vibrational freedom (much less disorder).
alternatives.correct_example: H₂(g) molecules can translate, rotate, and occupy a large volume with many possible positional and momentum microstates → high entropy. Si(s) atoms are fixed in a rigid crystal lattice; only vibrational motion is possible, giving far fewer accessible microstates. More microstates = higher entropy, so S°(H₂(g)) > S°(Si(s)).

wrong_examples: Saying H₂ has more entropy because it is a larger molecule — molecular mass is not the primary factor; phase (gas vs. solid) dominates.
Saying Si has more entropy because it is heavier — entropy correlates with phase and disorder, not mass alone.
common_traps: Entropy order: gas >> liquid > solid. Gas-phase molecules have translational + rotational + vibrational freedom → more microstates → higher S°. Solid atoms are position-fixed → fewer microstates → lower S°.
scoring_points.official_rubric: For a correct explanation: 1 point. $\text{H}_2(g)$ molecules are more highly dispersed than Si(s) atoms and therefore have higher absolute molar entropy. Silicon is a solid; its atoms are in fixed positions, less dispersed, with lower absolute molar entropy.

sample_responses.response: $\text{H}_2(g)$ molecules can freely translate and rotate throughout the container (more microstates). Si(s) atoms are locked in a rigid crystal lattice with only vibrational motion available. More dispersed = more microstates = higher entropy.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly contrasts the freedom of motion in gas vs. solid phase using microstate language.

sample_responses.response: H₂ has higher entropy because it has a larger molar mass.
sample_responses.commentary: Molar mass is not the primary factor; phase (gas vs. solid) dominates entropy.

parts.prompt: Calculate the value, in J/(mol·K), of ΔS° for the SiH₄ decomposition reaction.

scoring_points.description: For the correct ΔS° = +75 J/(mol·K): 1 point
wrong_examples: Not multiplying S°(H₂) by 2 — the stoichiometric coefficient is 2 for H₂ in the balanced equation.
Subtracting products from reactants — ΔS° = S°(products) − S°(reactants), not the reverse.
common_traps: ΔS°rxn = ΣnS°(products) − ΣmS°(reactants). Multiply each S° by its stoichiometric coefficient. ΔS° > 0 is consistent with producing more moles of gas (0 mol gas → 2 mol gas H₂).
scoring_points.official_rubric: For the correct calculated value: 1 point. $\Delta S^\circ = 2(131) + 18 - 205 = +75$ J/(mol·K).

sample_responses.commentary: Correct stoichiometric weighting of S° values; matches SG calculation.
Failed to multiply $S^\circ(\text{H}_2)$ by the stoichiometric coefficient of 2.

parts.prompt: The reaction is thermodynamically favorable at all temperatures. Explain why the reaction occurs only at high temperatures.
parts.scoring_notes: Thermodynamically favorable (ΔG < 0) means the reaction is spontaneous but says nothing about rate. The decomposition of SiH₄ has a high activation energy — the Si–H bonds must be broken, requiring significant thermal energy. At low temperatures, molecules lack sufficient kinetic energy to overcome the energy barrier (activation energy). At high temperatures, molecules have enough thermal energy to exceed the activation energy barrier, so the reaction proceeds at a practical rate. This is a kinetic (rate) limitation, not a thermodynamic one.

scoring_points.description: For correctly identifying a kinetic barrier (high activation energy) rather than a thermodynamic barrier: 1 point

required_elements: The reaction has a high activation energy (kinetic barrier).
At low temperatures, molecules lack sufficient thermal energy to overcome the activation energy — reaction is too slow despite being thermodynamically favorable.
alternatives.correct_example: Even though the reaction is thermodynamically favorable (ΔG < 0) at all temperatures, it has a high activation energy. At low temperatures, the reactant molecules do not have sufficient kinetic energy to overcome this energy barrier, so the reaction is too slow to observe. At high temperatures, more molecules have enough energy to exceed the activation energy (Arrhenius: k = Ae^(−Ea/RT)), so the reaction proceeds at a meaningful rate.

wrong_examples: Saying the reaction becomes thermodynamically favorable only at high T — the problem states it is favorable at ALL temperatures; the constraint is kinetic.
Saying the reaction needs heat to become spontaneous — ΔG is already negative at all T; the limitation is kinetics (activation energy).
common_traps: Distinguish thermodynamics (ΔG, feasibility) from kinetics (Ea, rate). ΔG < 0 means the reaction CAN happen; high Ea means it won't happen FAST unless temperature is high enough. Temperature increases k exponentially (Arrhenius equation).
scoring_points.official_rubric: For a correct explanation: 1 point. High temperature is required for reactant particles to have sufficient thermal energy to overcome the activation energy of the reaction.

sample_responses.response: Although the reaction is thermodynamically favorable at all temperatures, it has a high activation energy. At low temperatures, most molecules lack sufficient kinetic energy to overcome this barrier. High temperatures increase the fraction of molecules with energy ≥ Ea, allowing the reaction to proceed at a measurable rate.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly distinguishes thermodynamic favorability from kinetic barrier; matches SG reasoning.

sample_responses.response: At high temperatures the reaction becomes spontaneous.
sample_responses.commentary: The problem states the reaction is favorable at ALL temperatures; the constraint is kinetic (activation energy), not thermodynamic.

parts.prompt: A partial photoelectron spectrum of pure Si is shown below. On the spectrum, draw the missing peak that corresponds to the electrons in the 3p sublevel.
parts.scoring_notes: Si ground state: 1s²2s²2p⁶3s²3p². The 3p sublevel has 2 electrons. In PES, lower binding energy (easier to remove) = farther right on the x-axis. The 3p peak should be drawn to the right of (lower binding energy than) the 3s peak, at relative height corresponding to 2 electrons (same height as the 3s peak, both having 2 electrons).

parts.prompt: Using principles of atomic structure, explain why the first ionization energy of Ge is lower than that of Si. Then: a single photon with a wavelength of 4.00 × 10⁻⁷ m is absorbed by the Si sample. Calculate the energy of the photon in joules.
parts.scoring_notes:
IE comparison: Ge (Z=32, Period 4) and Si (Z=14, Period 3) are in the same group. Ge has more electron shells (4 vs 3), so its valence electrons (4p) are farther from the nucleus and more shielded by inner electrons. Greater distance + more shielding → weaker nuclear attraction → lower IE for Ge.

Photon energy: E = hc/λ = (6.626×10⁻³⁴ J·s)(3.00×10⁸ m/s) / (4.00×10⁻⁷ m) = 1.988×10⁻²⁵ / 4.00×10⁻⁷ = 4.97×10⁻¹⁹ J.

scoring_points.description: For a correct explanation of why IE(Ge) < IE(Si) referencing atomic structure: 1 point

required_elements: Ge has more electron shells than Si (Period 4 vs Period 3).
Ge valence electrons are farther from the nucleus and/or more shielded → weaker nuclear attraction → lower IE.
alternatives.correct_example: Ge (Period 4) has 4 electron shells, while Si (Period 3) has 3 electron shells. Ge's valence electrons (4p) are farther from the nucleus and experience greater shielding from inner-shell electrons. The effective nuclear charge experienced by the outer electrons in Ge is weaker relative to Si, so less energy is required to remove a valence electron from Ge → lower first ionization energy.

wrong_examples: Saying Ge has more protons — Ge does have more protons, but the increase in nuclear charge is outweighed by additional shielding from more electron shells.
Saying Ge is in a lower group — both Ge and Si are in Group 14; the difference is the period (row), not the group.
common_traps: Down a group: more shells → valence electrons farther and more shielded → lower effective nuclear charge → lower IE. The increase in Z is more than offset by increased shielding.
scoring_points.official_rubric: For a correct explanation: 1 point. Ge valence electrons occupy a higher shell (n = 4) than Si (n = 3), so the average distance between nucleus and valence electrons is greater in Ge, resulting in weaker Coulombic attraction and lower IE.

sample_responses.response: Ge (Period 4) has one more occupied electron shell than Si (Period 3). The valence electrons in Ge are farther from the nucleus and more shielded by inner electrons, so the effective nuclear charge they experience is weaker. Less energy is required to remove a valence electron from Ge than from Si.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly applies Coulombic attraction argument with shell distance and shielding; matches SG reasoning.

sample_responses.response: Ge has more protons so it attracts electrons more strongly, giving it a lower IE.
sample_responses.commentary: More protons increase nuclear charge but the extra shielding from additional shells dominates, lowering IE.

scoring_points.description: For the correct photon energy ≈ 4.97 × 10⁻¹⁹ J using E = hc/λ: 1 point
wrong_examples: Using E = hλ instead of E = hc/λ — energy is inversely proportional to wavelength.
Not converting wavelength to meters — λ = 4.00×10⁻⁷ m is already in meters; no conversion needed.
common_traps: E = hc/λ. Use h = 6.626×10⁻³⁴ J·s, c = 3.00×10⁸ m/s. Wavelength must be in meters. Shorter wavelength → higher energy (UV photon here: 400 nm = violet light).
scoring_points.official_rubric: For the correct calculated value: 1 point. $E = hc/\lambda = (6.626\times10^{-34})(2.998\times10^8)/(4.00\times10^{-7}) = 4.97\times10^{-19}$ J.

sample_responses.commentary: Correct substitution into $E = hc/\lambda$ with wavelength in meters.
Used $E = h\lambda$ instead of $E = hc/\lambda$; energy is inversely proportional to wavelength.
